content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 14, 2002


Paul Koretz, Assemblyman
California Legislature
Post Office Box 942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-0042

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-02-085

Dear Assemblyman Koretz:

Thank you for your request for informal assistance on behalf of potential candidates in new cities seceding from the City of Los Angeles. We are aware that your questions are important and of a sensitive nature.¹

You asked a variety of questions related to secession movement in Los Angeles. It is our understanding that the Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO) has not set a date for an election on this matter. You asked what laws apply to the elections. In addition you asked if there are contribution limits that apply to candidates seeking office in a new city that might be formed, should the secession election occur, and when and with whom these candidates would file.

The interplay between the various state and local laws that may apply can be complicated. For example, the Cortese-Knox Local Government Reorganization Act (Cortese-Knox Act)² provides a detailed framework for the creation, expansion, contraction and abolition of local government and special district boundaries. This collection of statutes sets forth the composition of and delegates authority to LAFCOs in 57 of California's 58 counties. Although some LAFCO-approved boundary change proposals can occur without a public vote, most--including the procedure known as secession--require voter approval. Activity that is within the

_______________________
¹ We are treating your letter as a request for informal assistance. Regulation 18329 sets forth the parameters for providing formal written advice. (Reg. 18329(b), copy enclosed.) The regulation provides, in pertinent part, that where formal advice cannot be rendered, the Commission may limit its assistance to the explanation, in general terms, of the requirements of the Act. (Reg. 18329(c)(4).)
² Enclosed is a copy of an August 30, 2001, staff memorandum discussing the status of the Act in relation to these elections, and the update to that memorandum, dated September 7, 2001.

File No. I-02-085
Page No. 2

parameters of the Cortese-Knox Act (see Govt. Code §§ 56100.1, 56300 and 56700.1) is outside
the Act and we, therefore, do not advise on these sections.

Moreover, the answers to all of your questions depend on many factors. However, we
can provide you the following general guidance at this time:

1) Generally speaking, both state and local laws apply to elections held throughout the
state. More specifically, however, state contribution limits apply only to elections for elective
state office, and not to local elections.

2) The Act requires the filing of campaign statements and reports by many persons. (See
Govt. Code §§ 84200 et seq.) Government Code Section 84215 addresses where these campaign
reports and statements must be filed. Government Code Sections 84215(d) and (e) provide:

"(d) County elected officers, municipal court judges, candidates
for these offices, their controlled committees, committees formed
or existing primarily to support or oppose candidates or local
measures to be voted upon in any number of jurisdictions within
one county, other than those specified in subdivision (e), and
county general purpose committees shall file the original and one
copy with the clerk of the county.

"(e) City elected officers, candidates for city office, their
controlled committees, committees formed or existing primarily to
support or oppose candidates or local measures to be voted upon in
one city, and city general purpose committees shall file the original
and one copy with the clerk of the city. These elected officers,
candidates, and committees need not file with the clerk of the
county in which they are domiciled."

Once LAFCO has made its determination regarding the boundaries and composition of
any new cities, the Commission will be able to determine which laws apply to an election. We
are also working with appropriate agencies, including the City of Los Angeles to formally
advise candidates and to educate interested persons with respect to the election about which you
inquire, if LAFCO determines that one should be held. Any guidelines that are developed will
be accessible to any candidates seeking guidance and to members of the public.

File No. I-02-085
Page No. 3

I hope this information is helpful to you and your constituents. We will forward a copy
of any informational materials we develop to you. If you have questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Very truly yours,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

Enclosures

sections.question: You asked a variety of questions related to secession movement in Los Angeles. It is our understanding that the Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO) has not set a date for an election on this matter. You asked what laws apply to the elections. In addition you asked if there are contribution limits that apply to candidates seeking office in a new city that might be formed, should the secession election occur, and when and with whom these candidates would file.
sections.conclusion: 1) Generally speaking, both state and local laws apply to elections held throughout the state. More specifically, however, state contribution limits apply only to elections for elective state office, and not to local elections. 2) The Act requires the filing of campaign statements and reports by many persons. Government Code Section 84215 addresses where these campaign reports and statements must be filed. Once LAFCO has made its determination regarding the boundaries and composition of any new cities, the Commission will be able to determine which laws apply to an election.
sections.question_synthetic: What campaign finance laws, contribution limits, and filing requirements apply to candidates seeking office in newly-formed cities resulting from Los Angeles secession movements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: State contribution limits do not apply to local elections; filing requirements for candidates and committees depend on whether they are county or city-level offices, with specific filing locations determined by Government Code Section 84215. The FPPC will provide detailed guidance once LAFCO determines the boundaries and composition of any new cities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter (not formal written advice per Regulation 18329). Document treated as request for informal assistance. Some sections quoted verbatim from Government Code. LAFCO determination was pending at time of letter, limiting specificity of advice. Enclosures referenced but not included in provided text.

QUESTION: You asked a variety of questions related to secession movement in Los Angeles. It is our understanding that the Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO) has not set a date for an election on this matter. You asked what laws apply to the elections. In addition you asked if there are contribution limits that apply to candidates seeking office in a new city that might be formed, should the secession election occur, and when and with whom these candidates would file.

CONCLUSION: 1) Generally speaking, both state and local laws apply to elections held throughout the state. More specifically, however, state contribution limits apply only to elections for elective state office, and not to local elections. 2) The Act requires the filing of campaign statements and reports by many persons. Government Code Section 84215 addresses where these campaign reports and statements must be filed. Once LAFCO has made its determination regarding the boundaries and composition of any new cities, the Commission will be able to determine which laws apply to an election.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides informal guidance to Assemblyman Koretz regarding campaign finance laws and filing requirements for potential candidates in cities that may be formed through Los Angeles secession. The FPPC clarifies that state contribution limits apply only to state offices, not local elections, and explains that filing requirements depend on LAFCO's determination of new city boundaries.
